# Questions in Livestream 151

Live Stream #151: Nine Myths About Spam

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=616" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:16</a> | Could gods like Osiris or Rama be based on real people later deified? | He leans more toward historical people being mythologized than toward an Egyptian divine-council explanation, while leaving room for uncertainty. | He says the deified-human view was common among early church fathers, and for Osiris he sees some evidence in the later veneration of King Djet's tomb at Abydos as Osiris's tomb. He cannot speak to Rama, and he does not wholly rule out divine-council ideas, but he thinks Egyptian religion's gradual development from animism, local gods, anthropomorphism, and state aggregation makes that model less natural for Egypt. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=843" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:03</a> | What does the Mount Ebal cultic site add to the case for the Exodus? | He says it fits a late-conquest chronology well and is hard to dismiss as anything other than a cult site. | Focusing on Adam Zertal's documented finds rather than ABR's claims, he says the site dates to Late Bronze IIB or early Iron I, which fits a late Exodus followed by a late conquest. He rejects the watchtower-base explanation because the structures contain piles of animal ash, which he says is what one expects from burnt animal offerings, and the site's narrow period of use makes it a useful supporting datum. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=1135" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:55</a> | How can someone resist Kabbalistic thinking when it seems appealing after converting to Christianity? | He recommends becoming deeply familiar with the Bible so counterfeit or extra-biblical ideas are easier to recognize. | He argues that Christians often think they know the Bible better than they do, so the answer is disciplined immersion until biblical thinking becomes instinctive. His analogy is counterfeit detection: bank tellers learn real money by handling genuine bills, and Christians should know Scripture so well that counterfeit teaching feels out of place. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=1523" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:23</a> | Did ancient Egyptian magic have a concept like mana or a limited energy supply, and how would it be replenished? | He says Egyptian magic did not work like mana; it was mostly sympathetic magic, with success thought to depend on alignment with the natural order. | He says mana is a Western magical idea, not an Egyptian model, and Egyptian spells did not draw down a caster's finite energy supply. The power lay in the relation between the object or focus of magic and the natural order; bigger effects were less likely to succeed because they deviated further from that order, but the caster did not run out of magical fuel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=1841" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:41</a> | What was the Egyptian title `kheret nesut`, and why did it emerge? | He describes it as a title for high-status court women or ladies in waiting, but says its exact origin and role are uncertain. | He translates the title approximately as "royal ornament," while noting that `kheret` can also have meanings connected with greenery or rushes and `nesut` means royal. It appears especially in the First Intermediate Period and Middle Kingdom, seems tied to high-status women connected with the court, and later gives way to other titles, but he says the evidence from tombs is not enough to define their precise function. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=2074" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:34</a> | What music do you listen to, if any? | He says his tastes are eclectic, including soundtracks, alternative music, anime music, and other styles. | He attributes his range partly to working in a department-store music section and later exploring music libraries. He names film and game soundtracks, anime music, alternative bands, Tangerine Dream, Evanescence, Metallica, international music, and some new age music, with a special affection for soundtracks like Dune, Blade Runner, Game of Thrones, and Pirates of the Caribbean. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=2489" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:29</a> | Does "spam" here mean the meat or Ron Wyatt fans? | He says the title joke means the meat. | He clarifies that the livestream title refers to the canned meat, not to Ron Wyatt fans. The exchange stays as a joke, with him saying they are not making jerky out of Wyatt fans and then returning to the super-chat queue. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=2602" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:22</a> | What are the flaws of purity culture? | He says purity culture had legitimate concerns but often handled sexuality in unhealthy or excessive ways. | He says purity culture was trying to keep teenagers away from real dangers such as premarital sex, drugs, and alcohol, but often used fear and prohibition in a counterproductive way. In his view, it should have taught the proper and covenantal use of good things, such as sex within marriage or responsible alcohol use, rather than making them attractive forbidden fruit. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=2980" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">49:40</a> | Did Egyptians need to be buried in Egyptian soil to reach the afterlife? | He says no; burial in foreign territory did not automatically block the afterlife. | He rejects the premise that Egyptians had to be buried in Egyptian soil, distinguishing Egypt's two lands from the broader earth. Egyptians buried in places like Canaanite garrison towns could still receive Egyptian-style burial; being away from a prepared tomb at home might reduce the trappings or status of the afterlife, but it did not prevent entry into it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=3187" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:07</a> | Can Christians celebrate nature, and what about non-demonic nature spirits? | He says nature can be celebrated but praise belongs to God, and any good spirit would redirect attention back to God rather than to itself. | He distinguishes celebration from praise: nature is not a person, and praise belongs to God. He allows that nature spirits could exist as governing placeholders in the created order, but says a spirit aligned with God would not seek worship or attention for itself, while one in rebellion might seek praise and should be refused. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=3583" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:43</a> | Is Mark 2:26 wrong when Jesus says Abiathar was high priest when David ate the showbread? | He says Abiathar was not high priest at the moment but became the major priestly figure associated with the episode and its aftermath. | He explains the phrase as an honorific retrojection: Abiathar survived Saul's massacre at Nob, escaped with the priestly vestments, and later became the well-known priestly figure associated with David. Like calling wartime Princess Elizabeth "Queen Elizabeth" in retrospect, the Gospel identifies Abiathar by the title readers knew him by, not by his office at that exact moment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=4056" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:36</a> | When in Ramesses II's reign would the Exodus occur, and why would Egypt not keep chasing Israel? | He places it roughly around the king's twenty-fifth regnal year and says Egypt was too reduced, especially in chariot strength, to chase them across Sinai. | He dates the Exodus around Ramesses II's twenty-fifth regnal year, plus or minus about seven years, because the crown prince was alive in year 18 but gone by about year 32. He argues Egypt would not pursue Israel far because roughly two-thirds of the chariot corps had been lost at the Sea of Reeds, the remaining chariots had to be spread across Egypt, and tracking a moving population through Sinai and later into the Jordan region would have been impractical. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=4485" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:45</a> | What is one of your guilty pleasures? | He says taking a day off, because running the channel leaves little real downtime. | He says a day off feels guilty because every break means a video is not filmed, a script is not written, a short is not made, or a thumbnail is late. Running the channel is full-time work with planned, tightly shaped content, so time off is rare even though he admits it is sometimes needed for health and sanity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=4628" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:17:08</a> | What are your general thoughts on Edgar Allan Poe? | He calls Poe an excellent writer with a strange life and death, and mentions liking his work. | His answer is brief: Poe was a great writer, "The Pit and the Pendulum" was excellent, and Poe's life and death were strange. He does not offer a long literary analysis, but he says he likes Poe's writing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=4683" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:03</a> | What are your thoughts on Jean-Paul Sartre and Ernest Hemingway? | He regards both as good writers, while noting Sartre's atheism and late-life conversion claims in the discussion. | He describes Sartre as a talented writer whose work reflected an atheistic and existentialist worldview, then mentions reports that Sartre became Christian near the end of life. For Hemingway, he says he appreciates the terse, staccato style more now than when younger, especially the skill of implying much while leaving things unsaid. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=5121" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:25:21</a> | What are your thoughts on H. P. Lovecraft? | He says Lovecraft was very good at creating atmosphere and mood, which he sees as central to Lovecraft's fiction. | He says Lovecraft's strength was atmospheric writing and notes Lovecraft's own view that mood is central to fantastic fiction. In that style, the monster and plot are almost secondary to the atmosphere being created, and that is what he likes about Lovecraft's work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=5233" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:13</a> | Can you provide commentary on Romans 7:14-25? | He summarizes the passage as Paul describing bondage to sin, the goodness of the law, and the need for rescue through Christ. | He reads the passage as beginning with the tension between the spiritual law and flesh sold into bondage to sin. Paul hates the sin he does, agrees that the law is good, yet cannot do the good he wants because sin indwells him; the section therefore describes the enslaving power of sin and the need for deliverance beyond human willpower. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=5959" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:39:19</a> | Are you going to watch `Testament: The Story of Moses`? | He says he might, but had not heard of it until the question was raised. | He gives a limited answer because the question is the first he has heard of the show. He says he might watch it and will see, but he has no formed judgment on it in the transcript. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=5996" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:39:56</a> | What do you mean by the Torah as Moses's memoir? | He compares it to day-by-day court writing later compiled into a single work by an editor. | He compares Moses's writing to Egyptian court day rolls, where daily events were recorded in small units over time. In his model, Moses produced a stack of wilderness-era notes, speeches, and records that were later sewn together by an editor into a cohesive book, which explains editorial introductions such as "this is what Moses said." |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=6203" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:43:23</a> | Why was it bad to remove the poles from the ark in Numbers 4:5-6? | He points to Exodus 25:15, where the poles are commanded to remain in the ark's rings, while saying the exact reason is uncertain. | He says the action was bad because Exodus explicitly commands that the poles remain in the ark's rings and not be removed. He does not claim to know the exact rationale, though he suggests a ritual-purity reason is possible, and frames the Numbers 4 problem as disobedience to an already stated command. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=6358" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:58</a> | How could Jephthah have escaped his vow? | He points to Leviticus 27 as a redemption provision for an extreme or hasty vow. | He reads Leviticus 27 as providing an escape clause for a difficult, extraordinary, or hasty vow by substituting a valuation payment for the person vowed. Since Jephthah's daughter was unmarried, he says ten shekels would have redeemed the vow, and that this lawful provision existed because of human weakness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=6697" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:51:37</a> | What is an executive summary? | He defines it as reducing a larger report or concept to a short paragraph for someone who does not have time to read the full material. | He explains the term from corporate usage: a secretary or worker might be handed a large document and told to reduce it to a single short paragraph. The point is to give busy executives the essential content without requiring them to read the full report. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=6786" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:06</a> | How would Egyptians smack-talk or insult people? | He discusses Egyptian pejoratives and says some insults were humorous, but the transcript only gives a partial survey. | Before answering insults, he addresses the King Tut aside, saying Tutankhamun's clubfoot appears genetic and not exclusive to him. For Egyptian insults, he says they tended to attack competence or merit rather than one's mother; he gives "scribe of excellent fingers" as praise and "clumsy fingers" as the insulting counterpart, while treating the answer as a partial sketch. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=7314" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:01:54</a> | How do we know Numbers 12:3 is not a later self-praising insertion? | He says the wording does not read like ordinary self-praise and fits the narrative style rather than a simple boast. | He argues the verse sounds like a backward-looking theological reflection because it calls Moses the humblest man on earth in third-person terms. Since people do not normally praise themselves that way, and since the comparison implies later reflection over Moses's life, he says it strongly reads as a scribal gloss after Moses. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=7507" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:05:07</a> | How can tarot cards be used to teach the gospel? | He says early tarot cards appear to have used ordered biblical imagery before later divinatory uses developed. | He says tarot originated in fifteenth-century France, that there is no evidence for earlier use, and that its original form was not fortune-telling with numbered cards. In his account, the older trump-card sequence contained biblical imagery from creation, Eden, sin, judgment, David, Messiah, crucifixion, resurrection, and new creation, functioning like a visual teaching sequence rather than random divination. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=7810" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:10</a> | What is your verdict on the filioque? | He rejects the filioque but says he also rejects the Eastern Orthodox reasoning based on eternal generation. | He explains the filioque as the Western addition that the Spirit proceeds from the Father "and the Son." He rejects that addition because he does not think the text affirms that double procession, but he also rejects the Eastern Orthodox appeal to eternal generation, so he calls the Eastern conclusion right for the wrong reason. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=8034" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:13:54</a> | Why is Calvinism not correct? | He says he is a three-point Calvinist, thinks Calvinism is more correct than Arminianism in some respects, but rejects some modern Calvinist formulations. | He says his own position accepts some Calvinist points and rejects or qualifies others. He warns that people should read Calvin before claiming or attacking Calvinism, because Calvin was more moderate than some modern forms and theological labels do not always match the founder's own views. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=8350" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:19:10</a> | How should 1 John 2:27 and 2 John 1:9-11 be understood together? | He says the received gospel is sufficient and true, and false teachers with a different gospel should not be allowed to teach the congregation. | For 1 John 2:27, he identifies the anointing with the received gospel, meaning believers do not need a later, supposedly better gospel. He then connects 2 John to false teachers who do not abide in Christ's teaching: such people should not be given a platform in the congregation, because welcoming their teaching would participate in their error. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=8779" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:26:19</a> | What is an example of Jesus being funny in the Greek? | He points to Jesus asking "what things?" on the Emmaus road and to the insult of calling Herod a fox as examples with humorous force. | He qualifies that Christ is a suffering figure and the humor is not always foregrounded, but he finds Luke 24 funny because Jesus knows exactly what happened and still asks the Emmaus disciples, "What things?" He also notes Jesus used mockery, including calling Herod a fox, which he treats as an insult with humorous bite. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=9151" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:32:31</a> | Is it biblical to suggest that the ends justify the means? | He says no; the means must justify themselves, and the ends-justify-the-means approach leads to unbiblical ethics. | He connects the phrase to utilitarian ethics, where whatever maximizes good can be treated as permissible. He rejects that as biblical because it has justified atrocities; good ends cannot sanctify evil methods, and the means themselves must be morally justified. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=9351" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:51</a> | Why distinguish between a christophany and a theophany? | He says the terms overlap but can be distinguished when identifying whether the appearance is specifically of Christ or more broadly of God. | He defines a theophany as a manifestation of God, often with a bodily form, and a christophany as a manifestation specifically of the second person of the Trinity. Because Old Testament cases are often hard to distinguish and christophany is a kind of theophany, he usually calls them theophanies unless the text gives a specific reason to be more precise. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=9501" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:38:21</a> | What helps the channel more, patrons or YouTube members? | He says both help in different ways: Patreon gives more net financial support, while YouTube members tend to be more engaged in the channel community. | He explains that YouTube takes about 30 percent from super chats and memberships, while Patreon takes much less, so Patreon gives better net financial support and allows more flexible monthly giving. YouTube members, however, tend to comment more, join live streams, and participate in community posts, so both forms help in different ways. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=9790" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:43:10</a> | When is the next Dungeons & Dragons game? | He says probably next November unless someone has a strong reason to schedule one sooner. | He says he is juggling many projects and cannot imagine doing another game soon without a compelling reason. His tentative expectation is the following November, while noting the previous speed game was good. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=9999" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:46:39</a> | What is your favorite Exodus movie, and why? | He names the 1950s Cecil B. DeMille film for its Egyptian archaeological detail, while criticizing the long romance section. | He prefers the 1950s Cecil B. DeMille Ten Commandments because it consulted Egyptologists and got many archaeological details right, including costumes, dance, the game of hounds and jackals, and a late-Exodus leaning. His main criticism is the long love-story section in the middle, which he says makes the movie less tight than it could be. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=10229" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:50:29</a> | Could the blocked movie commentary be put on Patreon, Dailymotion, Vimeo, or Rumble? | He says no; those platforms also face copyright enforcement, and Patreon can freeze accounts for copyrighted material. | He says other video platforms either already have or eventually will have copyright detection systems because of DMCA obligations, so moving the upload would not solve the problem. Patreon is especially risky because it can search for copyrighted material and freeze accounts, and commentary does not automatically avoid that enforcement. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=10451" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:54:11</a> | What advice would you give for starting a church class on physical evidence for the Bible? | He recommends using reliable scholarly materials and being cautious with internet apologetics sources that handle evidence poorly. | His main advice is to use good materials and be careful with internet claims. He specifically warns against weak apologetics sources and recommends recognized experts and resources such as Kenneth Kitchen, James Hoffmeier, John Walton, and broad Old Testament background volumes rather than evidence handled uncritically by popular apologists. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=10648" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:57:28</a> | What are your thoughts on Genesis 3 being the fall of Satan? | He thinks the reading is viable and connects it with Satan's expulsion and his own discussion of Satan as a covering cherub. | He says Genesis 3 can be read as the fall of Satan or at least the event that precipitates it. He connects the serpent with Satan through Ezekiel 28 and Revelation 20, and frames the fall as including Satan's expulsion from heaven after defeat by Michael. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=10741" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:59:01</a> | What are your thoughts on Isaiah 65:17 and memory in heaven? | He says the point is not that people forget former things, but that former troubles no longer matter in a healed state. | He reads "not remembered" as different from literal amnesia. Like childhood concerns that lose their force as circumstances change, former troubles will no longer dominate because heaven is a state of rejoicing, healing, and bliss; the memories are not necessarily erased, but they become irrelevant to the healed life. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=11056" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:04:16</a> | Do you accept late exilic authorship of Genesis 1-11? | He says no; the theory would not be devastating if true, but he does not find the evidence or arguments compelling. | He says late authorship would not create a major theological crisis for him, just as some late-authorship claims about Daniel would require adjustments without destroying doctrine. But he rejects the argument because he sees no evidence for it, especially no Old Iranian loanwords, while Genesis contains Egyptian and Semitic features that do not support a late exilic setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=11265" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:07:45</a> | What is meant in 2 Peter 2:11? | He explains the passage as saying judgment belongs to God, not even to righteous angelic powers. | He notes the passage is a long, complicated sentence that contrasts God's judgment of rebellious angels and the ungodly with his rescue of the righteous. The point of verse 11, in his reading, is that even angels greater in might and power do not bring reviling judgment before the Lord, so presumptuous humans should not treat judgment as theirs to wield. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=11671" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:14:31</a> | Did Israel learn household codes, gender roles, business practice, and similar laws from its history in Egypt? | He says Israelite law formation drew on multiple sources, including wilderness case law, Egypt, Mesopotamia, and local Canaanite contexts. | He pushes back slightly by noting that the Torah does include business-related principles such as fair weights. More broadly, he says many Israelite laws are case law from decided situations, with sources including wilderness experience, inherited material from Egypt, Mesopotamian law codes, and perhaps local Canaanite contexts, so Israelite law formation is mixed and complex. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=11804" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:16:44</a> | What ancient Near Eastern background helps with sacrifices and holiness in Leviticus? | He mentions priestly files, Hittite festival parallels, Egyptian purification practices, and the need for more source-based commentaries. | He says Leviticus has many possible ancient Near Eastern parallels, including priestly-duty files, Hittite wheat-festival parallels to Passover and Tabernacles, and Egyptian priestly purification practices such as washing, clean clothing, and restrictions during temple service. He laments that commentaries often recycle earlier commentaries instead of rebuilding from primary source comparisons. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=12112" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:21:52</a> | Do Israelites come from Canaanites, and are Amorites the same as Canaanites? | He says no; he identifies Israel's ancestry with Amorites and says Amorites were northern Mesopotamians, not Canaanites. | He says Amorites originated in northern Mesopotamia and migrated widely, including into Babylon, the northern Levant, Canaan, and even Egypt as the Fifteenth Dynasty. Because they are Mesopotamian rather than Canaanite in origin, he rejects equating Amorites with Canaanites and answers that Israelites do not come from Canaanites in that sense. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=12247" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:24:07</a> | Why are the Maccabees and the rest of the Apocrypha not in the canon? | He frames the issue as canon-law history, says Luther did not newly exclude them, and cites historical and authorship problems as barriers to full canonicity. | He says Luther did not remove the Apocrypha from the canon; rather, it had not been formally ratified as canon before the Reformation. He describes this as a canon-law oversight, noting that some church fathers quoted the Apocrypha but also quoted noncanonical works, and that Roman Catholic formal ratification came later at Trent, while Eastern Orthodoxy treated the books in a different wisdom-literature category. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=12660" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:31:00</a> | What are your thoughts on Denis Villeneuve's newer Dune films, and which Dune adaptation do you prefer? | He likes Dune broadly but says he did not care for Part Two because of major story and timeline changes, and he prefers Lynch overall. | He says he loves Dune and its adaptations, but Part Two compressed the novel's multi-year time span into less than a year and changed story elements he considers critical. He especially objects to Alia not being born and not killing the Baron, because that changes later continuity involving her relationship to the Baron's death. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=13706" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:48:26</a> | What are your thoughts on 1 Timothy 2:12? | He says women teaching in church is not the issue as such; he frames the passage around authority, order, and men failing to serve. | He says he personally has no problem with women teaching in church and thinks the passage is not simply a ban on women teaching. He frames the local problem as women doing all the work while men did little, and distinguishes teaching from exercising authority, especially public chastisement across age and social expectations. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=14611" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:03:31</a> | Why is Rehoboam's mother Naamah the Ammonite mentioned twice in 1 Kings 14? | He says the repetition draws attention to her Ammonite identity and its covenantal significance. | He says the repetition highlights that Solomon married an Ammonite woman and produced the heir to the throne through a line barred from the assembly for ten generations. In Kings, this is part of a trajectory from Solomon's disobedient intermarriages and idolatry to the later decline of the royal line. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=14910" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:08:30</a> | Why did Eve add "or touch it" to the divine command in Genesis 3? | He says changing God's wording changes the meaning and can become the first step toward getting around the command. | He notes that God had forbidden eating the fruit, not touching it, so Eve's addition changes the command's wording. Once the altered command can be tested by touching the fruit without dying, the door is opened to questioning and then violating the actual command; he compares this to other cases where adding words changes a biblical text's meaning. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=15494" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:18:14</a> | Was Neferneferuaten really Nefertiti? | He says we do not know; Nefertiti, Smenkhkare, or one of Nefertiti's daughters are possible, but the evidence is too limited. | He says the identity cannot be established from the available texts. The figure appears to be a short-lived Amarna-period king between Akhenaten or Smenkhkare and Tutankhamun, but the evidence is too thin to decide among Nefertiti, Smenkhkare, or a daughter of Nefertiti. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=15562" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:19:22</a> | Was Ramesses II overrated as a pharaoh? | He says yes as a military leader, but praises him as a prolific builder and architect. | He says Ramesses II fascinates him and was genuinely great as a builder, with major temples and monuments such as the Ramesseum, Abu Simbel, Abydos, and Karnak's hypostyle hall. But he calls Ramesses a poor military leader, so the military reputation is overrated while the building record is not. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=15694" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:21:34</a> | Which guitarist inspired you to play the SG, and when is the next Velocirabbit album coming? | He says the SG itself appealed for its versatility and affordability, and that a second Velocirabbit album has been started but not finished. | He says no guitarist in particular inspired the SG choice; the instrument's mechanics did. He liked its affordability, thin body, tone control, humbucker options, and ability to cover several roles, and he adds that a second Velocirabbit album has a working title but remains unfinished amid other projects. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=15977" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:26:17</a> | How should Exodus 4:24-26 be understood? | He says the issue is Moses failing to have his son circumcised, so Zipporah's act resolves a covenant violation. | He explains that Moses, raised as an Egyptian and leading Israel, should have kept the Abrahamic covenant sign by circumcising his sons, but had not done so, likely because the Midianites did not practice it. As Moses approached Egypt, God's anger increased; Zipporah acts as a faithful Gentile by circumcising the son and bringing Moses back into covenant obedience. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=16321" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:32:01</a> | Was circumcision only covenantal, did Egyptians practice it, and could it have originated in Egypt? | He says Israelite circumcision was covenantal but had practical effects, Egyptians did practice it, and a sign can be adopted into a covenant without originating there. | He says circumcision was required for Israelites as a covenant sign, though it also has practical disease-prevention effects. Egyptians practiced circumcision as early as the Fifth Dynasty and did it at varying ages, probably for sanitation or cultural reasons, so the Abrahamic covenant could adopt an already existing practice as its sign without needing the practice to originate with Abraham. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=16686" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:38:06</a> | How should 1 Corinthians 11:5-10, especially verse 10, be understood? | He says the reference to angels may mean heavenly beings or church messengers, so the verse has more than one possible reading. | He says "angels" can mean spiritual beings or human messengers traveling between churches, and he does not decide conclusively between them. If messengers are in view, the concern may be that visitors from other churches would observe local customs being rejected and question the church's order or soundness; he also says the passage appears culturally particular rather than universal. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=17065" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:44:25</a> | Did Moses write the Genesis patriarch stories, and were they oral tradition or compiled sources? | He thinks Moses likely recorded much of the material, but says the mix of oral and written sources before Sinai is hard to know. | He thinks most patriarchal stories were ultimately recorded by Moses, though they almost certainly had oral forms beforehand. Because preservation from Goshen is poor, he says it is hard to know whether Moses worked from wholly oral tradition, partial oral tradition, or documents the Israelites had carried with them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=17240" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:47:20</a> | Did Egyptians worship the sun, and why? | Yes. He says sun worship is understandable because the sun is obviously life-giving, dangerous, and overwhelmingly powerful. | He says Egyptians worshiped the sun as Re, with the solar disk called the Aten even before the Amarna period made Aten a distinct god. He calls sun worship understandable because the sun gives heat, light, and food, can kill through heat, and is the most powerful visible object around; the problem is not that the instinct is irrational, but that it is too small a concept of God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oGgvDwIod1U?t=17669" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:54:29</a> | What overlooked fact about John the Baptist stands out? | He says John was a Nazirite and highlights the vow's restrictions on hair, ritual purity, and the fruit of the vine. | He says John had taken the Nazirite vow like Samson, but unlike Samson he kept it. That meant not cutting his hair, avoiding ritual impurity such as contact with corpses, and not consuming fruit of the vine, including grapes, raisins, and wine. |
